text: The surgeon reflects the overlying structures, including the large and small bowel, away from the kidney, which is situated in the posterior aspect of the abdomen.

The surgeon dissects and identifies important anatomical structures, including the inferior vena cava (IVC) and the renal hilum, which contains a single vein and two arteries, clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the kidney beneath its layers of surrounding adipose tissue, revealing its position in the posterior aspect of the abdomen.
The surgeon uses ultrasound to precisely locate and confirm the position of the kidney tumor.
The surgeon excises the tumor from the kidney, ensuring a clear margin is present to retrieve all cancerous tissue.
The surgeon observes the removed tumor, and begins closing the defect in the kidney tissue where the tumor was excised.
The surgeon closes the entry point into the urinary system securely after it was inadvertently accessed during the procedure.
The surgeon oversews all major blood vessels to prevent significant bleeding upon restoring blood flow to the kidney.
The surgeon clamps the kidney vessels for sixteen minutes to minimize ischemic time, crucial for the patient with reduced renal function and only one remaining kidney.
The surgeon removes the clamps from the kidney arteries, visibly restoring blood flow to the kidney.
The surgeon applies large bolstering sutures to further close the defect where the tumor was removed, with no significant bleeding visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies blood clotting products to achieve hemostasis and uses bolster padding to compress and secure the defect tightly.
The surgeon retrieves the kidney specimen, visually confirming that there is no cancer present on any surface of the excised tissue.
The surgeon repositions all anatomical layers, restoring the kidney to its original location in the posterior part behind the abdominal cavity.
